name: EWING SARCOMA / TUMOR
definition: aggressive tumor with high rate of recurrence

demographics.Frequency: 3% of all pediatric cancers; 2nd most common primary malignant bone tumor in
demographics.Histo: crowded sheets of small round cells (10-15 µm), uniformly sized + solidly packed
demographics.Peak age: 10-15 years (range, 5 months - 54 years); in 95% 4-25 years; in 30% < 10 years; in
demographics.Location: sacrum (55%), lumbar (25%), thoracic > cervical; > 1 segment involved (8%)

dominant_finding: density of soft-tissue component similar to muscle (98%):
distribution: sacrum (55%), lumbar (25%), thoracic > cervical; > 1 segment involved (8%)
discriminating_features: 8-10 cm long aggressive lesion in shaft of long bone (62% lytic, 23% mixed density, 15%
mottled "moth-eaten" to permeative destructive lesion (76-82%) with wide zone of
geographic bone destruction with wide zone of transition (= poor margination) in 15%
penetration into soft tissue (56-80%) with preservation of tissue planes (DDx: osteomyelitis
early fusiform lamellated "onionskin" periosteal reaction (53-84%) / spiculated "sunburst" /

general: 8-10 cm long aggressive lesion in shaft of long bone (62% lytic, 23% mixed density, 15%

CT: density of soft-tissue component similar to muscle (98%):
linear channels of low attenuation extending through dense cortex ← tumor extension along
homogeneous (86%) low to intermediate (68%) SI on T2WI
heterogeneously high signal commonly in larger lesions ← hemorrhage + necrosis
visible connection between medullary canal + soft-tissue component (74%)
NUC: ↑ uptake on bone scintigraphy (blood flow, blood pool, delayed imaging)
PET: ↑ uptake of primary on PET with mean SUV of (a) 5.3 without metastases, (b) 11.3 with
MR: heterogeneous mass of low to intermediate SI relative to muscle on T1WI
intermediate to high SI relative to muscle on T2WI
Ewing sarcoma of vertebra:: large soft-tissue mass with invasion of spinal canal (91%)
Ewing sarcoma of rib:: primarily lytic / sclerotic / mixture of lysis + sclerosis
may spread into spinal canal via intervertebral foramen
5-10 cm rapidly growing solitary soft-tissue tumor
well-defined pseudocapsule (35%), infiltrative growth (45%), neurovascular involvement
extension to bone (25-42%): erosion of adjacent bone, cortical thickening, osseous
no replacement of fatty bone marrow by tumor
hypervascular lesion by angiography, CT, MR
